title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee March 17–18, 2026
summary: The FOMC minutes from March 17-18, 2026 reveal a committee grappling with conflicting pressures: AI-related disruptions to business models weighed on equities and rate expectations early in the period, while a Middle East conflict later drove a 50% surge in front-month crude oil futures, pushing near-term inflation expectations higher and shifting rate cut expectations. Market pricing now shows no rate cut fully priced until December, with roughly 30% probability of rate hikes through early 2027, though the Desk survey median still anticipated two 25 bp cuts this year.
key_points: Front-month crude oil futures prices increased approximately 50% over the intermeeting period due to Middle East conflict, with longer-dated futures rising significantly less, suggesting markets viewed the spike as potentially transitory
Near-term federal funds rate path implied by futures shifted higher, with a rate cut not fully priced in until December; options modal path shifted to no rate change this year
Probability of rate hikes through early 2027 increased to approximately 30% based on options pricing distribution
Desk survey median still showed two 25 bp rate cuts expected this year, though respondents pushed out timing slightly; some intelligence suggested further shifts toward fewer cuts after survey
Broad equity prices fell approximately 5% over the period, with software sector underperforming due to AI-related concerns; leveraged loan prices for software firms declined sharply
PCE inflation was 2.8% year-over-year in January, remaining elevated; one-year inflation swap rate rose nearly 50 basis points over period, while longer-term inflation compensation was little changed
Several central banks (ECB, BoC, SNB) previously expected to remain on hold or ease are now expected to hike rates modestly this year due to energy-driven global inflationary pressures
Standing repo operations reached $30 billion on one day (third-largest volume since inception), suggesting greater willingness to use operations following December framework changes
Reserves projected to average approximately $3 trillion through September; monthly pace of RMPs likely to be reduced significantly after April
why_it_matters: These minutes capture the Fed's assessment at a pivotal moment when geopolitical risk (Middle East conflict) collided with existing tech-sector volatility (AI disruptions), creating opposing forces on inflation and growth outlooks. The divergence between market pricing (no cuts, 30% hike probability) and the Desk survey median (two cuts) highlights significant uncertainty about the policy path. For macro traders, the shift in global central bank expectations (ECB, BoC, SNB now expected to hike) could strengthen the dollar and complicate the Fed's decision-making.
macro_context: The committee noted real GDP continued expanding at a solid pace, the unemployment rate was little changed with low job gains, and PCE inflation remained elevated at 2.8%. The oil price shock created a classic stagflationary risk: higher near-term inflation with uncertain growth impact. The one-year inflation swap rate rose nearly 50 basis points, but forward measures beyond one year were little changed, suggesting markets viewed energy-driven inflation as temporary while remaining alert to second-round effects.
market_structure_context: Treasury market liquidity diminished modestly in line with yield volatility increases but continued functioning well. Money market conditions remained broadly stable; the effective federal funds rate stayed 1 basis point below IORB, and overnight reverse repo usage was minimal except at month-ends. Standing repo usage reached $30 billion on one day with high Treasury settlement volumes, with interdealer repo rates not much above the standing repo rate—interpreted as increased willingness to use operations following December 2025 framework changes.
important_numbers: ~50% - front-month crude oil futures price increase over period
~5% - broad equity price decline over period
~$30 billion - standing repo operations usage (third-largest volume)
~$3 trillion - projected average reserves through September
~30% - probability of rate hikes through early 2027 (options-based)
~50 bps - rise in one-year inflation swap rate over period
1 basis point - effective federal funds rate spread below IORB
